turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in No. 95-6, Norfolk and Western Railway Company v. William J. Hiles. Mr. Phillips.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case involves the proper interpretation of section 2 of the Safety Appliance Act, which is quoted on page 2 of the petitioner's brief, and which states that it shall be unlawful for a railroad to use on its line -- excuse me -- any car not equipped with couplers coupling automatically by impact, and which can be uncoupled without the necessity of men going between the ends of the cars. In this case the respondent, a switchman at the rail yard in St. Louis, Missouri, injured his back while attempting to pull a drawbar into alignment that had become misaligned almost certainly as a consequence of having been disconnected from a prior car on a curve.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Phillips, was the drawbar technology essentially the same at the time the statute

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Justice O'Connor. I don't think there's much in the way of a significant advance since 1893.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so at the time that the statute we're reviewing was adopted, it was necessary for the drawbar to be physically adjusted at times between the cars.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Justice O'Connor, I believe that's correct. As the D.C. Circuit said in the Lewis case, that if you accept the notion that any misaligned drawbar violates the Safety Appliance Act, then every railroad has been in noncompliance with that act since 1898 when it went into effect, which means that every railroad has been subject since 1898 to $100 fines for every such violation, which might have actually saved the Federal Government its budget problems today, but clearly would have done so on the back of the railroads in ways I don't think Congress intended. What we have here is an injury that falls far afield from what Congress had in mind in 1893 when it acted to protect railroad workers from the possibility of being crushed between railroad cars as a consequence of the old-fashioned link and coupling devices used.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could you just refresh my memory? If does he have? Is there a workmen's compensation scheme?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: There's the Federal Employers' Liability Act that would have been available to him had he chosen to bring that action. That, of course --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can he still bring that action?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I would assume at this stage it's too late, that the statute of limitations would have run. It seems to me it was a conscious choice by the employee in this case to bring this action exclusively under the Safety Appliance Act and to do so to obtain the benefits under State law, the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you have to show some negligence under FELA?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: He would have to show some negligence under FELA, and respondent has steadfastly avoided any effort to attempt to do that in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, if there's an on-the-job injury scheme?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Not to my knowledge, no, Your Honor. The required negligence -- it's the only scheme that stands out in this particular way. On the other hand, of course, unlike most workers' compensation schemes, where there is a fairly fixed cap on the recovery, there's no similar cap on the recovery under FELA. As a consequence of that, employees tend to get significant recoveries.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Jones Act picks up on the same thing. So, it's for seamen as well as railroad workers.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: The seamen have the same -- that's correct, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mentioned the Lewis case and as far as fines are concerned, you're certainly right, but what do you make of this statement in Lewis that the railroad's duty is, as far as the worker is concerned and the worker's injury -- it's a duty not just to provide proper equipment, but to guarantee its performance. That seems to say the -- there can be no fine against the railroad but there is a guarantee to the worker.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I guess I would have two responses to that, Justice Ginsburg. First, I think to the extent that that suggests that there might be some potential claim as a matter of negligence or even a claim under the Safety Appliance Act where the only evidence is a malfunction and no evidence put forward by the railroad as to why the malfunction occurred, that is, to show that -- simply a case of a misaligned drawbar or an unopened knuckle -- sure, there could be recovery under those circumstances, although I wouldn't expect that to happen square the broader understanding of that language with the statutory scheme because it's reasonably clear to me that in order to recover under - - through FELA for a Safety Appliance Act violation, it has to be a Safety Appliance Act violation. You cannot stretch the Safety Appliance Act to cover situations that it wasn't designed to cover recompense through FELA as a technical matter. It seems to me you'd have to conclude that a misaligned drawbar is a violation of section 2.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying politely that Judge MacKinnon was incorrect to the extent that he said that the -- that there is a guarantee of the performance?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I think you can justify that statement in the very limited sense in which this Court has in the past recognized that if all the proof you have liability under those circumstances. To the extent that that's what his statement means, I don't have any quarrel with it. To the extent he goes beyond that, you're right, Justice Ginsburg. I was being polite in suggesting I didn't agree with him. railroad, there could be liability. What would be the theory or the explanation of liability there?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Why would there be liability in that situation?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, this Court's decisions suggest that there are essentially two ways to demonstrate a violation of the Safety Appliance Act: either there has been a malfunction of the coupler or there has been a defect in the coupler. In the malfunctioning situation, what the Court says is, we will presume, if it malfunctioned, that it had something to do with an equipment failure. But what the Court said quite plainly in Affolder was that that presumes in the first instance that the couplers were set to operate normally, and if the couplers were not set, in that case by the way, opening of the knuckle -- in our case I submit by way of aligning the drawbar so that the knuckles would in fact connect -- then it seems to me, as the Court said in Affolder, we would have a good defense under that circumstance. So, really the way the particular problem arises simply is a technical kind of pleading problem. If there

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's correct, Mr. Chief Justice. We have no malfunction and we have no defect. employee to go between the ends of two cars, tracking the end language of section 2 of the Safety Appliance Act, that then any injuries that arise under those through FELA. And our answer to that is that that simply a statute that imposes operational restrictions on how railroads go about their business. What the language about going between two cars does is fairly describe the type of coupler that Congress meant to require in 1893. And a little bit of history here goes a long way to giving content to that particular phrase. What we knew at the time was that in 1893 automatic couplers were fairly new to the scene, and Congress did not want to mandate any particular type of coupling and uncoupling mechanism. It simply wanted to describe the type of a device that it wanted implemented by 1898, and therefore what the -- as the Court again in the D.C. Circuit said in the Lewis case, what we know is that that independent requirement about going between cars is not itself an independent prohibition under the act. Part of the reason we know that it can't be really a significant independent requirement under the act is it doesn't even modify, by its own terms, the fact of coupling. Now, this Court has applied that language to the coupling as a reasonable, I think, interpretation of probably Congress' intent:, but it seems quite unlikely that Congress would have meant to make going between cars the ultimate sort of sine qua non of a violation of the

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then there's a thing that juts out from the car.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Drawbar.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it has a knuckle on the end.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the thing that juts out is a drawbar.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And sometimes you have to have a little give because it moves back and forth when they go around curves. And sometimes when it gets over here, it gets stuck over here.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then the person has to go between the car and move it back here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: -- when he does that, obviously, he doesn't have to be right between the cars. The cars can be quite far apart.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why didn't somebody think of just putting a spring on it so the spring makes it come back?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I assume -- I mean, there have been experiments made over the past 80, 90 years trying to come up with a mechanism to make it spring back, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Actually my law clerk found one in the Car Locomotive Cyclopedia for 1974. They have four pictures, and it says, this is a device. It keeps a free coupler centered in relation to the end. If the coupler is swung wide when disengaged from another, the device instantly returns it to center position.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that what we're talking about? They have four pictures of it.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I assume that would be one of the - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he also found a place where -- he was quite ingenious to find all this stuff, but he found a place where they're doing research right now. It says 4 proj ect. They're -- but my point is that this material I'm reading to you makes it sound as if the problem is a design problem. It isn't just a maintenance problem.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: No. I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if it's a design problem and not just a maintenance problem -- I'm sure Congress didn't want to stop people from going in betwreen cars to maintain oil - -

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- rust-free paint, but they did have in mind designing.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, that goes to the second broad theory that the respondents put forward, which is that you really have a duty to come up with some system to realign these drawbars.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: The statute says --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, why not? If 4 percent of people's lost time is lost because of the -- I'm not saying that I -- how I view this case.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: No, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm simply trying to figure out, is it more like maintaining, you oil the thing, or is it more like designing a better coupler? So, I look at this stuff and say, well, 4 percent lost time, the Association of Railroads says we're going to do research. This is a - - they have four drawings of springs. It sounds like design and if it's design, isn't that the kind of problem Congress had in mind?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I don't think it's the kind of problem Congress had in mind because we know that Congress dealt specifically with drawbars in the Safety Appliance Act itself and in section 5 said, set up a drawbar at a certain height. So, Congress knows the difference between coupling mechanisms and the drawbar, and Congress dealt with the drawbar. Now, with respect to - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how do we know that the drawbar is not part of the coupling device?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, we can --we know that Congress at least viewed them differently because section 2 talks about coupling couplers and section 5 talks about drawbars. So, Congress at least had in mind the possibility of the two being distinct, and as a consequence of that, that seems to me a reasonable basis for answering Justice Breyer's point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but they had -- the old system

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it had a link at the end.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the link went inside another drawbar, I take it, called a pocket, and then you dropped a pin in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, you'd think the coupling system was that whole thing, wouldn't you? The thing that sticks out, the link, the pin, and --

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I'm not trying to draw too fine a distinction here. All I'm saying is that Congress clearly had the two different things in mind. This is an automatic coupling requirement, not an automatic realignment of the drawbar requirement. And the fact that Congress discussed specifically drawbars simply creates an inference to me that Congress didn't go as far as what the respondents have proposed as far as the obligations under the Safety Appliance Acc. I think the more fundamental - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, excuse me. I thought your fundamental response is simply even if that were so, this is not what Congress meant by the necessity of men going carries with it an independent substantive requirement. Now, the respondents do make the alternative kind of a duty to guarantee that drawbars can be realigned regardless of what it takes, that we have a technological And ail.1 was trying to say to Justice Breyer is I don't think that's what this statute ever had in mind. I don't see anything in the language of the statute that would support it, and indeed, the absence of an independent requirement of having to go between the cars is the complete - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Argument. It becomes part of that same argument.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I may have parsed their argument too finely for that purpose, but I do think the point remains the same which is there is no duty on the Justice Breyer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How many injuries were there last year, if you know, approximately caused when people went between cars to realign misaligned drawbars? Do we know the answer to that question? the footnote in our brief. As I recall, it depends on which years you pick. One year there was one death and a handful of injuries.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But is that -- I know that -- I do recall I had some numbers on that, but I didn't know that that was related to going between cars to cure the problem of misaligned drawbars.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I don't think anybody collects the data on that close a point. I think what they do is they collect data on coupling problems, and then whatever coupling problems may -- presumably you could have an that - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The statute in any case requires the necessity of going between the ends of the vehicles. Right?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. the vehicles closed while the person is in there.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But your point is, I take it, that there's no necessity of going between the ends of vehicles within the meaning of the statute --

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Within --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- with this coupling system.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's correct, within the meaning of this statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And this individual wasn't hurt by reason of being between the two cars at all. He strained his back as I gather, trying to straighten it out.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's right. If he had --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It could have happened if the car had been up on a mount.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Absolutely. If he had been asked to bring a coupler in and put it on the end of the drawbar and picked up the coupler and injured his back, he would have exactly the same injury and exactly the same situation, but there wouldn't be a serious argument that that violated the Safety Appliance Act. Again, that's a situation where the Federal Employers' Liability Act would apply. about this technology and whether it exists. The most recent evidence I saw from the AAR was that on the devices that have these kinds of springs, something in the neighborhood of 30-some percent were the only -- were in operation -- were actually working, even on -- where they have those devices. For some reason -- and I don't frankly know the technical explanation for it, but for some reason these springs don't hold up very well and in that sense probably pose at least as much of a risk of having people go in there - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is this in the record, Mr. Phillips?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: This is in the D.C. Circuit's opinion in the Lewis case. There's a discussion of this evidence. That's all. I just mentioned it for that purpose.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The reason that I find it quite relevant -- and I'm uncertain about how to proceed -- is if in fact there are a lot of people who are hurt by this and they have to go between the cars to realign the drawbars and you could cure it at very little expense with statute which was worried about going between the bars and being hurt for coupling.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I would go back --

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I would o back to Justice Scalia's point because I think you cannot et to that sta e unless you conclude that there is an independent prohibition a ainst oin between cars. And that I don't think the purpose or the lan ua e of this statute will sustain. And as a consequence of that, to the extent that they et back in order a ain, it should be dealt with across the street and not by the Court here. At the end of the day, what we have here is an injury that is nowhere near the kinds of injuries that Con ress had in mind when it enacted the Safety Appliance Act in 1893. This is an injury that is fully compensable if there has been ne li ence by the railroad under the Federal Employers' Liability Act. In addition to that, of course, the railroad would have the opportunity under those circumstances to demonstrate contributory ne li ence and thereby reduce the employee's recovery as a consequence of that contributory ne li ence. I think that is a balanced scheme and that this Court ou ht not to unbalance that scheme by ivin the Safety Appliance Act an unduly broad interpretation. For

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Phillips, I think Judge MacKinnon was writing as of -- what -- 1983, and he said that after 90 years that the automatic realigning devices, as distinguished from automatic coupling devices, are still in the experimental stage and had been installed on less than 1 percent of the railroad cars.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was as of 1983 he was writing, and what you're saying is that since that time there has not been any significant change.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: As I understand it, there has not been any significant technological advance that would eliminate what problems existed in the past. That's my understanding. Again, none of this happens to be in the record, though. If there are no other questions, I'd reserve the balance of my time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Phillips. Mr. Mann, we'll hear from you. ON. BEHALF OF THE RESPONDENT

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I have a photograph that may assist the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where in the record -- is that a photograph of something that's in the record?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: That's an exhibit, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: An exhibit.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Yes. And what I want to point out to the Court is that the rail industry has dealt with only one aspect of coupling and uncoupling. They have dealt with the uncoupling process. They have provided a lever here that you see which allows the coupler to uncouple without the necessity of anyone going in between the cars. What they haven't addressed is the coupling procedure. They have not touched the coupling device to prevent it from going back and realigning. And, Justice Breyer, the passenger service in this country has that type of device in effect. They use it and they use it without major problems. The freight industry --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do we know that from the record?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I was involved in the case at this - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do we know that from the record what you just --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Not the record. It's in my brief.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The point is that this is a performance standard statute. The Congress was not concerned with how the industry attempted to prevent employees from going in between the cars. They said to the industry, take care of the problem in coupling and uncoupling. Take care of that problem. Do not allow the employees to go between the cars in either process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Mann, I assume when that coupling deyice is initially manufactured, somebody had to be there to put it on. Right? And the car that that car would first be coupled to was somewhere down maybe miles away, but it was somewhere down the railroad tracks.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And one could say that the person who installed that coupler was going between the two cars, namely, between the car he manufactured and the car it would ultimately be coupled to. Now, do you think that's what the statute refers to?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he was going between the cars.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Only the - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay. So, we have to draw a line uncoupling. The maintenance testing and inspection of the car -- there is no problem here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right. Now, is the process of coupling or -- you say the pi'ocess does not begin with the installation of the coupler.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: It does not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why does it begin with the setting of

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Because it will not engage unless it’s set properly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It will not engage if it's not installed either.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: But that is not covered under the statute, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I agree but I don't see why setting the arm is covered either. It seems to me what the statute addresses is the immediate action of coupling and uncoupling which is physically risky because in the old days the employees faced the necessity, which is what the statute refers to -- the necessity of going between the cars while they were close together and in the process of coupling.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: It's not only the proximity, but in the preparation of the coupling. first place is preparation?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No. It's not in the preparation of coupling. No, sir. That could be in a shop somewhere.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well -- MR. MANl): That's certainly not in the preparation of coupling in my judgment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. I see. So, installing it wouldn't have been --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- wouldn't have been covered.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But as soon as he installs it, he moves the arm to the proper central position. Then he is covered. That's --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I disagree. No, sir. Not until the cars are on the tracks - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Within some proximity.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Some proximity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. Okay. So, we're talking about how much proximity?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I don't think it matters --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How much proximity was there here?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I think there was a car length or maybe two, but it doesn't matter. The fact is if the employee has to go in preparation of the coupling procedure, it doesn't matter what time or how long that time is in his work day. The purpose is to prevent that employee from going in between the cars for coupling or uncoupling so that if that car sits on the track, say, even an hour, so long as it is in preparation of the coupling procedure, I submit to you, Justice Scalia, it's

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I agree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it seems to me under this statute the reasonable line to draw it is when the cars about to be humped together in order to effect the coupling or are being pulled apart in order to disengage it.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, I submit, Justice Scalia, the immediate preparation of that car so that that could occur would be encompassed there. knowing why we should interpret the statute - - the word coupler -- as including the drawbar. I'm not sure it does.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Justice O'Connor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They may well be different things. They are dealt with differently in the statute. And one could envision a coupler as not including the drawbar as such.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: But the drawbar is all part and parcel -- there's the draw head, which is the actual coupling device, and then it is attached in one solid piece of equipment. It's not separate in any way. It's one solid piece of equipment -- this whole drawbar -- and part of that is the head of the drawbar. in the different sections of the statute that Congress saw these as different things.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I -- they dealt with particular parts of the whole drawbar, but they're not different. It's all part and parcel of the same piece of equipment. It's not -- you can't detach it. 1 mean, you could -- legally you could take the whole part off, but you don't take one off

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems like what they were after was a system to replace the old method where somebody had to go-between the cars to put the pin in to couple the cars.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that it was this joinder business where they come together and the old pin used to have to go that they were talking about

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- not the drawbar that swings back and forth of necessity to allow the train to turn.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: But that pin could never have been placed in the train unless they were together.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but it's sort of the -- you know, the head bone is connected to the neck bone is connected to the backbone, et cetera.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but the most common thing was this business of having to put in the pins. That's what they were focusing on. They didn't want people to have to go in there and physically drop in the pins. of technology at that time as you have today. Congress -- it is a performance standard, Justice O'Connor, and being a performance standard, Congress didn't care how you do it. Congress said to the industry, do it, protect the employee in this procedure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could you have done it in 893? In other words, we've heard talk about what the technology technology in 1893? I gathered from Judge MacKinnon's opinion that he was assuming the technology didn't exist in 1893 so that in fact one - - on your theory of the statute, the railroad could not have conformed in these situations.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: But, Justice Souter, Judge MacKinnon stated, misaligned drawbar or a closed coupler is sufficient to establish liability under section 2.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm talking about what he was saying about the technology.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The technology.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the technology exist in 1893 to -- in effect, to conform to the standard of the statute as you read it?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Not at that time, but I think that that - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why would Congress have passed a statute which assumed a technology that didn't exist? If that's what it wanted to do, why wouldn't -- w'hy wouldn't it have been a lot simpler for Congress to say, look, the railroads are absolutely liable without fault whenever anybody gets hurt between two cars? If that's what they wanted to accomplish --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No. mechanically possible and at a time when it was not mechanically possible to guarantee the kind of degree of safety that you claim is inherent in the standard of obligation?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I think there are -- there may be two answers to that. First of all, technology was just emerging. Congress didn't know nor did it concern itself with how this performance standard was to be performed. during that procedure, whatever that procedure is. And I could envision, Justice Souter, of having a large rod standing on the outside of the car and simply pushing it, but the industry hasn't provided that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let me ask you another question. It's a simple question and it goes to what I'm seeing in that picture. Why doesn't the -- why isn't it possible for that lever, which the picture shows, to align the drawbar prior to coupling?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, I'm not saying the technology could not be devised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It just looks as though that's what it could do and maybe it isn't.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, here it's attached. It doesn't

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. devise it. It's not unlike the old Finto cases where it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Before 970, there was --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: With the Interstate Commerce

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For a century it had the authority to us .

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, I don't know that. I'm not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't know the contrary either.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I -- that's not in the record anywhere that there was no enforcement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know. That's exactly what I'm pointing out. This thing has been going on for a century and we have no indication not only of anybody recovering previously because of this safety defect, but of any Federal agency trying to enforce the safety requirement.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The -- two things. One is the Court has addressed injuries occurring in this type of situation, not specifically the misalignment, but in other - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Mann, apart from what the courts have done, in response to Justice Scalia's question, isn't the Lewis case highly relevant because that was an agency regulation?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was a regulation that said it's okay to use this hook procedure.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, do I understand your reasoning with respect to the drawbar to be inconsistent with the ok y to h ve this regul tion th t permitted the hook procedure, even though it involved going between the c rs t le st with p rt of one's body?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The only p rt of one's body -- if you rec ll, Justice Ginsburg, the hook c me into pl y fter the coupler w s opened p rt w y. This is not tot lly effective nd efficient bec use it doesn't lw ys open it nd the employee stood beside the c r, of course h d to re ch his rm inside, nd pull it out. Th t's th t piece of equipment th t w s used in Lewis. And the court -- I me n, I c n't t ke issue with the decision of the court, but the court further s id in th t c se th t if there is mis ligned dr wb r, then th t is covered s viol tion under section 2 of the ct. And th t's just wh t We h ve here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could you tell me little bit bout your rule of c us tion nd tell me wh t the rule of c us tion is? Suppose the employee sees the mis ligned dr wb r the c rs nd stumbles nd hurts himself. Is there li bility?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: No. I don't think there's li bility is there liability? MR . MANN : Until he - - I think there's a super- all - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there's cause in fact. If it hadn't been for the drawbar, he -

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- wouldn't have moved and wouldn't have stumbled.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The violation exists.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, there must -- so, you must be saying that there is some requirement of proximate causation between the defective operation of the mechanism and the injury.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: There - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You would agree with that.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Yes. And I further state that there already was the because there was the necessity of the employee to even have to go in between. The violation already occurred. Now, whether or not there's causation for recovery is a different issue, of course, but as far as the violation of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One obvious reason --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But in --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One more quick. In this .case, as I understood it, your position is that the - - whether or not the drawbar was stuck was irrelevant, but it seems to me that -- am I wron about

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: It's not irrelevant. The Court has held in a number of cases that you do not have to establish a defect in a car to prove violation of section 2. Affolder was one, a O'Donnell case, Carter case. There are several cases, and the Court said that over and over. And that's the ist of the railroad's ar ument here, that you must show a defect before you can recover. But if -- if -- the performance requirement is not there, this statute says that's a violation because it's up to the carrier to protect the employee. Otherwise, you're oin to continue to have employee injuries and deaths.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what exactly is the performance requirement that you deduce from the statute?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I am sayin that the performance requirement is that in every case, not point. So is Carter. O'Donnell, they said it doesn't matter that you expect too much of the coupler. Justice Kennedy's question, in this case wasn't the injury he hurt his back?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: In pushing, he -- what he attempted to do

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: -- Justice Breyer, is push it back in concerned about, this procedure. It's so dangerous, and - -

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Yes, but not everything is of course a problem as - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any indication that what Congress was worried about was the fact that it was heavy and you might hurt your back pushing it, any part of this device?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I submit it was broader than that. They wanted --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there anything that suggests it was at least as broad as to catch that? I mean, is there anything about bad backs and pushing things?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Not bad backs per se.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, pushing. Pushing. You hurt your muscle because you pushed. it wouldn't be necessary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I'm actually thinking too is one reason why this might not have come up before before the railroad commissions is no one has ever been hurt by this in a way in which Congress was worried about. That is, their footnote says one person is killed and 36 injured in all coupling and decoupling accidents, of which pushing drawbars or having anything to do with misaligned drawbars must be a subset. And so is there any information that anyone has been hurt in the manner that Congress foresaw by crushing or hands or something to do with it being a car other than just being a heavy weight to push? Ever?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The Federal Railroad Administration has a breakd wn f th se that were injured in m ving equipment, th se that were injured in n n-m ving equipment, th se that were killed in b th --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what is the answer then as t h w many pe ple were injured r killed in dealing with misaligned drawbars?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I can't -- I d n't kn w. it. We c uldn't find it.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: I d n't kn w. But they d keep statistical data and whether it's br ken d wn that specific, I can't answer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: S , then is the -- if I th ught that really what I'm trying t distinguish here is between what I'd call r utine maintenance f things that ccasi nally cause tr uble and seri us design pr blems -- and a seri us design pr blem has t d with numbers and the kinds f injuries C ngress th ught ab ut -- then w uldn't I have t decide this against y u because the rec rd isn't -- d esn't have that inf rmati n and I can't get it en ugh?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or w uld I decide in y ur fav r n s me presumpti n? I d n't --

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, I - - if y u determined that implies that failure to do it hurts somebody and it's possible to redesign your way out of it. So, in that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Mann, the safe that they're concerned about is not back safety. I don't see how you win if it's a design defect either. For all we know, the ultimate design that comes that enables this arm to be adjusted from inside the railroad car with just as much physical effort as it takes to do it from outside or from the side of the car so that he doesn't have to go between the car, but he still has to push just as hard. He would have hurt his back just the same way.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: He would not have had a violation of section 2. There may be some other violation but not of section 2.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Mann, in O'Donnell the Court says on page 389, we hold that the Safety Appliance Act requires couplers which, after a secure coupling is effected, will remain coupled until set free by some purposeful act of control. It seems to me that's far short of standing for what you say it stands for.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: But in --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It then goes on to say, negligence is not required, which everybody concedes here.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: And it further, I think, Mr. Chief Justice, states that the railroad cannot avoid liability depending upon how difficult it will be for them to make a coupler that works in all situations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. It says the act requires a coupler, once it couples, stays coupled, but that really

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, if you're drawing the the whole drawbar, and I don't see how you can distinguish --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in Affolder, there was also dicta in which the Court said, of course, the result assumes the coupler was placed in a position to operate on impact. The railroad would have a good defense that the

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Very logical of the Court because they had developed this technology to allow it to open and close without the necessity of the employee going between, but they haven't addressed the other part of that statute, and that is the coupling part. The uncoupling part they did address.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Mann, I want to get back to Palsgraf. You seem to assume that if there has been a violation of the act, anything that occurs as -- with but Now, is that true?

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The Court has held - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just simply because the act has been violated even though the injury has nothing to do with the act's violation

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The Court has held that it's absolute liability

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose that it's misaligned, he has to go between the cars, and he gets bit by a snake

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Super-intervening cause. It's not caused by the - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why isn't his bad back a supervening cause.-

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, you're talking about recovery

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, you say supervening cause, but

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: -- liability --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Supervening causes are -- there is a chain of factual causality here.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Sure, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He pushed the bar. That's the supervening cause.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: The violation has already occurred.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's the same case in the snake case.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: Well, it's similar because it is -- the violation already exists. The question of whether or not there's recovery is a different aspect of liability.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's part of what we're talking about I assume. snake bite. We have the immediate issue of pushing the drawbar which they have not used their technology to correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he might have had the push it from inside the car even if there had been no safety violation. They might have devised a new system in which would have huirt his back the same way. covered.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the statute doesn't say in preparation for coupling. It doesn't say that. I think you're grafting something on that isn't in the statute.

turns.name: LAWRENCE M. MANN
turns.text: There's the Lewis case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Lewis case is a court of appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't see why we should make a here. of the Court. That case, the employee was literally standing on this coupler --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your time has expired, Mr. Mann. Thank you. Mr. Phillips, you have minutes remaining.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, unless there

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well. The case is submitted.
